channel.title: Marty Identity Protocol Blog
channel.description: Technical insights, implementation guides, and business perspectives on the Marty Identity Protocol and verifiable credentials.

item.title: Revocation Strategies Compared: CRL, OCSP, StatusList, and Accumulator
item.description: Credentials get revoked. MIP's revocation model supports four strategies with different privacy, latency, and offline trade-offs.
item.dc:creator: Dr. Anika Patel

item.title: Conformance Testing: Proving Your MIP Implementation Works
item.description: MIP ships a conformance test suite with valid and invalid fixtures. Here's how to use it to validate your implementation.
item.dc:creator: Marcus Webb

item.title: MIP and Open Badges: Verifiable Education Credentials
item.description: Open Badges 3.0 meets MIP — how the protocol brings trust governance to education and workforce credentials.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Chen

item.title: Holder Binding: Beyond Biometrics
item.description: How do you prove the person presenting a credential is the person it was issued to? MIP supports cryptographic, biometric, and knowledge-based binding.
item.dc:creator: Dr. Anika Patel

item.title: Offline Verification: Design Patterns for Disconnected Environments
item.description: Airports, border crossings, and field operations can't always reach the cloud. MIP's offline patterns maintain security without connectivity.
item.dc:creator: Marcus Webb

item.title: Building Trust Registries at Scale
item.description: Who is authorized to issue which credentials? Trust registries answer this question — and MIP provides a framework for building them.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Chen

item.title: Post-Quantum Readiness in Identity Protocols
item.description: Quantum computers will break RSA and ECDSA. MIP's algorithm-agnostic Trust Profiles are designed for the transition — here's how.
item.dc:creator: Dr. Anika Patel

item.title: MIP JSON Schemas: A Walkthrough for Implementers
item.description: MIP ships with 35 JSON Schemas covering every protocol entity. Here's how to use them for validation, code generation, and API contracts.
item.dc:creator: Marcus Webb

item.title: Introducing the Marty Identity Protocol
item.description: Today we open-source MIP — a vendor-neutral specification for cryptographically verifiable digital identity management. Five primitives, one protocol, zero lock-in.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Chen

item.title: Writing Cedar Policies for Identity Governance
item.description: A practical guide to writing Cedar authorization policies for MIP — from basic access control to complex approval workflows.
item.dc:creator: Marcus Webb

item.title: SD-JWT Selective Disclosure: A Deep Dive
item.description: How SD-JWT enables selective disclosure without zero-knowledge proofs — and where MIP uses it across Credential Templates and Presentation Policies.
item.dc:creator: Dr. Anika Patel

item.title: Compliance Profiles: Bridging Standards and Regulation
item.description: ICAO, AAMVA, EUDI, Open Badges — every standard has its own rules. Compliance Profiles map them to MIP primitives so you configure once and comply everywhere.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Chen

item.title: Flows: Orchestrating the Identity Lifecycle
item.description: Apply, Approve, Issue, Present, Verify. Flows are MIP's fifth primitive — the user-facing product that ties everything together.
item.dc:creator: Marcus Webb

item.title: Zero-Knowledge Predicates in Identity: What, Why, and How
item.description: Proving you're over 21 without revealing your birthday. ZK predicates in MIP enable privacy-preserving identity verification at protocol level.
item.dc:creator: Dr. Anika Patel

item.title: Deployment Profiles: From Design to Production
item.description: Trust Profiles and Presentation Policies are design-time. Deployment Profiles are how they get to devices, kiosks, and APIs in the real world.
item.dc:creator: Marcus Webb

item.title: EUDI Wallet Readiness: What the Regulation Actually Requires
item.description: The EU Digital Identity Wallet regulation is coming. Here's what it means for your identity infrastructure — and how protocol-first design helps.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Chen

item.title: Presentation Policies and the Art of Minimum Disclosure
item.description: Verifiers shouldn't ask for everything. Presentation Policies formalize exactly what's needed — and nothing more.
item.dc:creator: Dr. Anika Patel

item.title: Credential Templates: Designing What Gets Issued
item.description: A credential is more than a signed JSON blob. Credential Templates define the schema, claims, validity, and disclosure rules that make credentials meaningful.
item.dc:creator: Marcus Webb

item.title: Cryptographic Trust Anchors: A Primer for Identity Systems
item.description: X.509 chains, CSCA/DS certificates, and DID documents — the cryptographic foundations that make verifiable credentials actually verifiable.
item.dc:creator: Dr. Anika Patel

item.title: The Business Case for Credential Portability
item.description: Vendor lock-in costs enterprises millions in re-verification. Portable credentials built on open standards eliminate that waste.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Chen

item.title: Trust Profiles: The Foundation of Every Identity Decision
item.description: Before you can verify a credential, you need to decide who to trust. Trust Profiles formalize that decision as configuration, not code.
item.dc:creator: Marcus Webb

item.title: Why Digital Identity Needs a Protocol, Not Another Platform
item.description: Platforms create lock-in. Protocols create ecosystems. Here's why the identity industry is ready for a protocol-first approach.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Chen
